1: The divine self-introduction "I am God Almighty (ʾĒl Šadday)" marks Abram's third major theophany and the first use of this divine name. ʾĒl Šadday is used six times in the patriarchal narratives and thirty-one times in Job; the LXX renders it pantokrator (Almighty). Its etymology is debated — possibly "God of the mountains" (from šad, mountain, cf. Akkadian šadū), "God the Sufficient One" (from šādad, to be powerful), or "God who nurses" (from šad, breast, emphasizing sustaining provision). "Walk before me faithfully and be blameless (tāmîm)" — the same word used of Noah in Genesis 6:9; it means wholeness/integrity of life, not moral perfection. The covenant-ethics demand accompanies the covenant-promise.
2: "I will make my covenant (ʾĕttĕnāh bĕrîtî)" — the verb is "give/set" rather than "initiate"; the covenant was established in Genesis 15; here it is being specified, expanded, and sealed with the circumcision sign. The framing "greatly increase your numbers" (ʾarbeh ʾôtĕkā bimʾōḏ mĕʾōḏ — double-mĕʾōḏ intensifier) repeats the offspring promise at the moment of the covenant's formalization. In ANE suzerain-vassal treaties, the great king's self-introduction ("I am...") followed by his past benefits and future commitments is the standard treaty preamble — Genesis 17 follows this structure closely.
3: "Abram fell facedown" (wayyippōl ʾaḇrām ʿal-pānāyw) — the prostration posture (hištaḥăwāh, bowing to the ground) is the standard ANE posture before a superior: a king, a divine being, or a person of greatly higher status. The same posture appears at every major Abrahamic theophany and throughout prophetic accounts of divine encounter (Ezekiel prostrates at each theophanic vision, Ezekiel 1:28; Genesis 3:23; Genesis 9:8). The physical act of falling facedown is the somatic response to the overwhelming of normal human standing before the divine presence.
4: "You will be the father of many nations (ʾab hămôn gôyîm)" — the covenant declaration anticipates the name change of v5. The phrase hămôn gôyîm (multitude of nations) is embedded in the new name Abraham: ʾaḇrāhām is a phonetic expansion of ʾaḇrām with the sound of hāmôn (multitude) embedded — the name carries the promise in its syllables. The covenant explicitly extends beyond Israel: "many nations" encompasses Ishmael's twelve rulers (v20), the sons of Keturah (Genesis 25:1-4), and ultimately the Gentile inclusion in the Abrahamic covenant that Paul argues from in Galatians 3:8,29.
5: Abram → Abraham — name changes in the ANE mark a new covenant identity or social status. The change from ʾaḇrām (exalted father) to ʾaḇrāhām (father of many/a multitude) embeds the covenant promise in the name itself: every time the name is spoken, the promise is reiterated. The same identity-marking function underlies Jacob → Israel (Genesis 32:28), and Sarai → Sarah (v15). In later rabbinic tradition (and Hebrews 11:8-12), the name change is treated as a covenant act with lasting significance — after Genesis 17, the narrative never reverts to "Abram."
6: "I will make you very fruitful; I will make nations of you, and kings will come from you" — the royal-lineage element of the Abrahamic covenant. Kings descending from Abraham through Sarah (v16: kings from her also) traces the line to David (2 Samuel 7:12-16) and Solomon. The "kings from you" fulfillment includes the entire Davidic dynasty and ultimately, in NT reading, Christ the messianic King (Matthew 1:1: "son of David, son of Abraham"; Revelation 17:14: "King of kings"). The promise of kings from a nomadic patriarch with no biological heir is the kind of audacious promise that the ANE audience would have recognized as extraordinary.
7: "I will establish my covenant as an everlasting covenant (bĕrît ʿôlām)" — the same ʿôlām covenant-language as the Noahic covenant (Genesis 9:16) — permanent, unconditional on the human partner's sustained compliance. "To be your God and the God of your descendants after you" — the covenant formula "I will be your God / you will be my people" appears in full form here for the first time; it recurs throughout the OT as the summary of the covenant relationship (Jeremiah 7:23; Genesis 11:4; Genesis 30:22; Ezekiel 36:28; Genesis 37:23,27; Zechariah 8:8; 2 Corinthians 6:16; Revelation 21:3). This formula is the canonical shorthand for the entire covenant relationship.
8: "The whole land of Canaan, where you now reside as a foreigner (gēr)" — the narrative irony is pointed: Abram is a gēr (resident alien without land rights) in the very land promised to him as an everlasting possession. The tension between present landlessness and future possession is the constitutive experience of the patriarchal narrative: Abraham buys only a burial plot (Genesis 23) and Jacob returns to Egypt. The gēr status defines the patriarchs' relationship to Canaan until the conquest — possession is covenantal before it is territorial. The same tension defines Israel in Egypt (a nation of gērîm) and, the NT argues, the church as "aliens and strangers" (1 Peter 2:11) awaiting the promised inheritance.
9: "As for you (wĕʾattāh), you must keep my covenant" — the human obligation of the covenant is formally introduced. The covenant of Genesis 15 was unilateral (YHWH alone walked through the pieces); Genesis 17 introduces the bilateral sign: YHWH's covenant commitment is responded to by the human covenant marker. The covenant has two movements: divine promise-pledge (vv1-8) and human sign-pledge (vv9-14). The covenant is not earned by the sign, but the sign is not optional — its absence is a covenant violation (v14: "cut off from his people").
10: "Every male among you shall be circumcised (yimmôl)" — the circumcision command uses the Niphal of mûl (to circumcise), a technical cultic-legal term. Circumcision was practiced by Egyptians (Herodotus, Histories 2.36-37), some Semitic peoples (Jeremiah 9:25-26 lists the circumcised nations alongside Israel: Egypt, Judah, Edom, Ammon, Moab, Kedar), and Arab tribes — but not by Philistines or Assyrians. Israel's use of circumcision as covenant-identity marker in this ANE context is distinctive for its specific theological rationale (covenant sign) rather than merely hygienic or initiation practice.
11: Circumcision as sign (ʾôt) — the same Hebrew word used for the Noahic rainbow (Genesis 9:12,13,17), the Sabbath sign (Exodus 31:13), and the blood on the Passover doorpost (Exodus 12:13). Signs in the OT are concrete, visible markers that point to an invisible covenant reality — they do not create the reality but mark and confirm it. Paul's argument in Romans 4:11 — "Abraham received the sign of circumcision as a seal of the righteousness he had by faith while he was still uncircumcised" — exploits the temporal order of Genesis 15 (faith/righteousness, v6) before Genesis 17 (circumcision sign): the sign follows the reality, not the other way around.
12: "Every male among you who is eight days old must be circumcised" — the eight-day specification for infant circumcision is distinctively Israelite. Ishmael's circumcision at thirteen (v25) represents a pattern associated with Arabian tribes and the Islamic tradition of circumcision near puberty. The eight-day rule was established for male infants born in the household; adult males circumcised upon joining the household (vv23,27) receive the sign as adults. The eighth day in Levitical law also marks the completion of the dedication period for priests (Leviticus 8:33-9:1) and the minimum age for sacrificial animals (Leviticus 22:27) — the eighth day is the threshold of covenantal viability.
13: "My covenant in your flesh (bĕrîtî biḇśarĕkem) is to be an everlasting covenant" — circumcision is an embodied covenant: the physical mark on the body represents the covenant claim on the whole person and the covenant people. The flesh becomes the bearer of the covenant sign — the most intimate possible location for a public marker. The prophetic interiorization of circumcision ("circumcise your hearts," Deuteronomy 10:16; Genesis 30:6; Jeremiah 4:4; Genesis 9:26) does not abolish the physical sign but argues that the physical sign must correspond to an inner reality. Paul argues in Colossians 2:11 that Christ's "circumcision" (his death) produces the interior circumcision of the heart that the physical sign pointed toward.
14: "Any uncircumcised male... will be cut off (nikkĕrĕtāh) from his people" — the karet (cutting-off) penalty is a divine judgment form distinct from judicial execution. The OT uses karet for violations of covenant boundary markers: eating leavened bread at Passover (Exodus 12:15,19), violating the Sabbath (Exodus 31:14), and various cultic transgressions (Lev 17-20 list numerous karet offenses). The karet penalty is exclusion from the covenant community — which in the OT context means exclusion from the divine presence and covenant-protection. The wordplay is pointed: the word for covenant-cutting (kārat bĕrît) and the word for cutting-off as punishment (kārat) share the same root.
15: Sarai → Sarah — both names derive from the root śārar (to rule/be a prince); the forms are variant spellings of "princess/noble woman." Unlike Abram/Abraham (where the meaning changes significantly), the name-change for Sarah is subtle — it marks covenant identity rather than semantic expansion. She is now the explicitly designated covenant-mother, not merely Abram's wife. In the Ugaritic texts (šrrt) and Akkadian (šarratu), the cognate means "queen" — the name's royal connotation anticipates the promise of v16 that "kings of peoples will come from her."
16: "I will bless her and will surely give you a son by her. I will bless her so that she will be the mother of nations; kings of peoples will come from her" — Sarah receives a direct divine blessing parallel to Abraham's (vv1-8); the covenant-promise is not merely to the patriarch but to the matriarch equally. The royal-lineage specification ("kings of peoples will come from her") narrows the covenant-heir line to Sarah's son specifically, excluding Hagar's line. This specification explains why Ishmael — though blessed — cannot be the covenant heir: the royal-messianic line runs through Sarah.
17: Abraham's laughter (wayyiṣḥaq) — the same verb as Isaac's name (yiṣḥāq). The name Isaac embeds three laughs: Abraham's incredulous laugh here (v17), Sarah's disbelieving laugh in Genesis 18:12, and the laugh of joy at Isaac's birth (Genesis 21:6: "God has brought me laughter, and everyone who hears about this will laugh with me"). The name "he laughs" names the child of impossible promise through the human laughter that greeted that promise. The inner monologue — "Will a son be born to a man a hundred years old? Will Sarah bear a child at the age of ninety?" — reflects the physiological reasoning that makes the promise humanly unimaginable.
18: "If only Ishmael might live under your blessing (lûlēy yišmāʿēl yiḥyeh lĕpānêkā)!" — Abraham's pastoral intercession for his existing son. The particle lûlēy expresses a wish the speaker does not expect will be granted — Abraham is asking that YHWH's purpose be satisfied through Ishmael rather than through the impossible promise. The request shows that Abraham has bonded with the son he has raised for thirteen years (Ishmael is now 13, v25) and that he finds the covenant's focus on an unborn son difficult to absorb. The request is not rejected but redirected: Ishmael will be blessed (v20), but not through him will the covenant run (v21).
19: "Your wife Sarah will bear you a son, and you will call him Isaac (yiṣḥāq)" — Isaac is named by YHWH before birth, as Ishmael was (Genesis 16:11). Only Ishmael and Isaac among the patriarchs are divinely named before birth — a marker of their special covenant significance (compare Jeremiah named before birth, Jeremiah 1:5; John the Baptist, Luke 1:13; Jesus, Luke 1:31). "I will establish my covenant with him as an everlasting covenant for his descendants" — the contrast with v20 (where Ishmael receives blessing, not covenant) is the exegetical foundation of Paul's argument in Galatians 4:21-31: the children of promise vs. the children of flesh are distinguished by covenant-status, not ethnic descent.
20: Ishmael's specific blessing — "twelve rulers (šĕnêm ʿāśār nĕśiʾîm)" — enumerated in Genesis 25:13-16 as the twelve sons of Ishmael who became the leaders of Arabian tribal confederacies. The twelve-ruler structure mirrors Israel's twelve tribes (twelve sons of Jacob), though through one son rather than through covenant multiplication. "A great nation (gôy gādôl)" — the same designation used for Israel (Deuteronomy 4:6,8; Genesis 26:5). YHWH grants Ishmael national greatness without the covenant-line — the excluded line is not condemned but given a generous alternative destiny.
21: "But my covenant I will establish with Isaac, whom Sarah will bear to you by this time next year (lammôʿēd hazzeh bašānāh hāʾaḥeret)" — the specific timeframe "by this appointed time next year" (lammôʿēd, at the appointed/fixed time) is the same word used in 18:14 and Genesis 21:2. The term môʿēd (appointed time/festival) has covenantal weight: it is the word for the "appointed feasts" of YHWH in Lev 23. The covenant heir arrives "at the appointed time" — YHWH's timing is not arbitrary but fixed by covenant commitment.
22: "God went up from him" (wayyaʿal ʾĕlōhîm mēʿal ʾaḇrāhām) — the divine withdrawal after theophany is the standard narrative marker for the end of the encounter. The same vertical language appears at the Bethel theophany (Genesis 28:12-13: YHWH "above" the ladder; the ascending and descending angels), and anticipates the later Ascension language (Acts 1:9-11). In the ANE context, divine appearances were understood as the deity descending to the human level for communication and returning to the divine realm afterward; the directional language is theophanic convention.
23: "On that very day (baʿeṣem hayyôm hazzeh) Abraham took his son Ishmael and circumcised them" — the phrase baʿeṣem hayyôm hazzeh (in the bone/substance of this same day) is a rare emphasis-formula that marks epoch-boundary events: the Exodus departure (Exodus 12:17,41,51), the entry into the promised land (Joshua 5:11), and the Jerusalem siege beginning (Ezekiel 24:2). Abraham's immediate obedience — on the same day he received the command — is the narrative's commendation of covenant faithfulness without delay. The prompt obedience contrasts with the hesitation of Lot in Genesis 19:16.
24: Abraham was ninety-nine years old when he was circumcised — an adult male circumcision at that age is a physically costly act. The covenant-sign is not received passively but involves genuine physical sacrifice by the covenant-head. The adult circumcision of Abraham (as opposed to the infant circumcision established for subsequent generations) establishes the covenant at the maximum cost: the founder of the covenant-family receives the sign at the age when it is most demanding, not most convenient.
25: Ishmael was thirteen when he was circumcised — his age at the covenant-sign corresponds to the pattern associated with initiation-circumcision in Arabian tribal traditions. The Islamic tradition of circumcision as a mark of male religious identity derives partly from Ishmael's example, though the specific age of thirteen is not universally followed in Islamic practice (the tradition varies from infancy to puberty). The contrast with Isaac's eighth-day circumcision (Genesis 21:4) is the contrast between the covenant-heir (eighth day, the Israelite sign) and the blessed-but-excluded line (initiation timing, a different cultural pattern).
26: Both Abraham and Ishmael circumcised "on that same day" — covenant solidarity; father and son enter the covenant sign together. The household unity of the circumcision event echoes the household-salvation pattern of Noah's ark (all who entered were saved together) and the Passover blood on the household doorpost (the household unit as covenant beneficiary). The covenant-sign is both individual (each person's body) and communal (the household enters together).
27: "Every male in Abraham's household, including those born in his household or bought from a foreigner, was circumcised with him" — the covenant sign extends to all males of the household regardless of ethnicity. The slaves purchased from foreigners (ben-nēkār, foreign-born) are circumcised as full members of the covenant household; their ethnic origin is no barrier to covenant inclusion. This expansive covenant community — defined by household membership and circumcision rather than ethnic descent — is the principle Paul will cite (Galatians 3:28-29) for the Gentile inclusion in the Abrahamic covenant through faith.
